Bought this fog machine for our Halloween haunted house. Old one is still working, but over ten years old. The remote on this works (for me) to a distance of about 50feet. It pumps out decent fog in our large space. I like that you can use remote, or manual switch. I’ve found the lights to be kinda pointless.. their range is limited to about ten feet even on brightest setting. And the “white” light is more greenish. A much better lighting solution is to get a laser type stand alone. Buy this for its fogging, not it’s lighting.

The SM7b has been my dream mic and I was so happy to finally get it. However, if you are just switching from USB to XLR mics for the first time like me, it is a lot more of a journey/expense than you may think!

It has a beautiful build and the most attractive mic out there by far. Very boomy and I have a deeper voice for a woman so I did sound pretty nice with it. However, to fully make use of it you need to have a Cloudlifter as the SM7b is notorious for having extremely low gain.

Materials needed/What I got:

For basics you need a sound mixer, XLR cable, and optional but highly recommended, a cloudlifter.

I got the Elgato Wave XLR (rn $159.99) but for the XLR cable a high quality one is recommended. Cloudlifter is $149 rn and the SM7b is $399.99. A popular mixer that is also used is the Focusrite ($179 rn).

Therefore $159.99 (mixer) + $399.99 (sm7b) + $149 (cl) = $708.98+ starter price not even including the XLR cable.

So keep this in mind if you plan on using this and that you are ready to jump into this investment. It is worth it if you have the money/resources needed!

I'm an elementary band director and I've had several students show up with these instruments. Please trust me - I've probably had 20 kids with Mendinis and have a lot more experience than one parent who (at the time of a review) had a positive outlook. Band directors all cringe at the name of Mendini, or any other of the cheap Amazon instruments. They. Do. Not. Last.

1. Really, they do not last. I've never had a student play on one for more than a year. They break all the time. Constantly. Weekly, sometimes. Because they are cheap, noname instruments, they can't be fixed by repair shops because replacement parts don't exist and/or don't work. Many repair shops refuse to even try to fix it because even if it could be fixed, the metal is so thin, cheap, and brittle that it will either break or bend right back. Kids get frustrated when their instrument is never working and then quit.

2. The tone is horrible. Absolutely horrible. This is not me being a picky band director - the kids even notice it and point it out. Nobody wants their kid to be singled out because they are doing some poorly - and it's not even the kid's fault!

3. The tuning is extremely off. This is noticeable by the kids as well. It's to the point where the instrument is playing a completely different note than the one that it is supposed to sound like. No amount of adjustment from the kid or teacher will fix this. Your child will stick out and be embarrassed.

4. No resale value. The instrument will not last long enough to even have value after a few months, but even if it did, it will not hold value. Good instruments are atill valuable years after - people are still playing on instruments from the 40s. In a few months, you will be stuck with a perpetually broken instrument that either you will pour money in to trying to fix it, or that is so broken it can't be fixed and then you are flat out of that money.

I understand; band is expensive. But you could take this money and find a used instrument - a good brand- and end up with something that will WORK! If that's not an option, talk to your school's band director to see if they have school instruments available.

Trust me - this is NOT band director approved. We call Mendinis 'instrument shaped objects' because that is exactly all they are.

If you ignore the rest of my advice, at least do your kid one favor - don't get them a weird color of this instrument. It will only help all the other kids identify whose sound is bad even faster.
For such a crude interpretation of a pickup it works quite well. I am impressed with the sound I am able to achieve.

I've had such fun with my OU-6, I figured this would be the cat's whiskers... solid top vs. the plywood top on my other Oscar Schmidt. NOT. First, the sound. Just not there... not alive. Muted, dead. Second, there were some finish problems (uncommon with O S instruments) AND third, and this was a killer, it was waaaaay out of tune at the 12th fret. My OU-6 is dead on pitch, so this was a surprise. SAD DISAPPOINTMENT. Returned. Oh yeah, fourth. The O S logo on the headstock was crooked, must have been done by a drunk. Sad. I had such high hopes. This WAS to be my most beautiful uke... vine fretboard inlay, etc.

These reeds are utter crap! I've used Rico #2 reeds for a number of years because of their ease of use and that you don't have to blow too hard for them to work correctly. I went through this entire 3 pack in one concert and a rehearsal a few day later.

The first reed made it through to the intermission of my concert and barely would work anymore. I changed it out during intermission to the second reed in the pack. Worked great for about 3 songs and then it too started not to work, and by the end of the concert was really difficult to blow through. A few days later I had a rehearsal so I tried using the 2nd reed from the 3 pack thinking I might have been tired towards the end of the concert. About 20 minutes into rehearsal the 2nd one died completely. So I changed to the 3rd reed from the pack. This one work for about an hour and then I could only get squeaks out of it. Ridiculous and frustrating!

Sure I've run into one every once in a while that was bad, but not all 3 from the same pack... maybe it was just a bad lot, but I'm not very happy. Now I have to order more before my next rehearsal. I'm sad to see that Amazon is endorsing reeds from this seller.

After I got a convertible standing desk riser, I needed something to sit beside my desk to hold my laptop as there isn’t much room left on my desk anymore. I needed it to be adjustable in height so I can lift it up higher when I use the standing position and lower it back down when I sit to work.

This was fairly easy to put together. It only took me about 7 minutes to fully assemble. What I noticed right away is how “sharp” the edges are, like it lacked finishing or was just cheaply made. That became more apparent as I screwed on the knobs. The plastic and metal pieces all have sharp edges that could have been refined better, but it’s not super major. They’re just not nice to touch and the knobs are a bit hard to twist because they’re poking into your hand the whole time. And the tripod base is made of very thin metal, so I definitely wouldn’t use this outdoors or anywhere the floor isn’t level and there’s no danger of big gusts or other environmental risk of it being knocked over.

I also wouldn’t plan on toting this around. If you intend to carry it in the bag it came with, you’d have to break everything completely down and I’m not sure the tray part even fits in that bag. And you’d have to take the tray off the top extension tube, which would require unscrewing those four screws every time. Not a great design for portability.

The foam piece is super thin and had a big gouge or manufacturing crease in the middle. If you were counting on the foam cradling an expensive projector or other piece of equipment, I’d suggest buying another piece of foam and cutting it to size.

It works for my needs, but I don’t plan to move this around or use it for anything other than my laptop. Overall it’s just okay in my opinion. Nothing terrible, nothing great. Three solid stars.

First of all, even though the description says this microphone is a "studio professional" mic, don't compare it to a "real" studio professional microphone!! For an inexpensive alternative, it does fine. The audio quality is actually pretty decent on this mic but becuz there is virtually no compression, it picks up everything. If you are going to use this for speaking and want to do things like Youtube videos, etc, you will need a pop filter so it doesn't pick up all the background noise and ambient noise. It is easy plug and play - you do not need a phantom power source and that is perfect for someone that just wants to do an easy in home set up. The stand is rather clumsy and falls apart easily - again, not something that is critical to its function, but rather an annoyance and the jack that plugs in seemed a little loose on mine. It did work, but just did not seem like a tight fit like it should have been. This mic works for my purpose and after all, you are not out an arm and a leg on price. I received this product for free/discount in exchange for a fair and honest review. All opinions are completely my own and if this review was helpful to you, please hit the "yes" button at the end of the review!

I bought this to use with my Baby Taylor...great sound, but I did have an issue with it fitting correctly. Would give it four stars, but the spring had a sharp bend in it near the pu and that kept it from fitting tight, square, and level with the strings. I straightened the bend the best I could and it fits better, but not perfect. I am concerned about the spring breaking if I try to adjust it more. Don't know why it would have been bent like that, unless it has been 'used/abused' before. Would return it for another, but need it right away.

Seems to be made of quality materials and I love the cool green color. However, the hinge and spring don't seem to work as easily as a similar capo that we have. The hinge seems to bind a little which could perhaps be caused by the paint that is used to make this green. And the spring is super tight so you have to squeeze pretty hard in order to open the capo wide enough to put it on and to take it off. Perhaps these issues will improve over time once the hinge and spring are broken in a bit more. Otherwise, the capo really seems to do its job well holding the strings down against the frets no matter where you place it along the guitar neck. With that stong spring inside it doesn't fail.

I recieved this product at a discounted price for my honest and unbiased review.

I love this guitar, especially for the price! The pickups on the electric end are a little weak but for the money, this guitar is way worth it! I use mine for songs that frequently switch back and forth between acoustic and electric and it makes for one hell of a conversation piece when I show people my collection. Word to the wise, though, being as thin as it is the acoustic sounds very shallow without the pickup. I had mine set up at a local guitar store to put my preferred strings on it, check the frets, and fix the intonation (they said it wasn't bad for just coming from the factory) as it was recommended for several other of the guitars that Ktone makes and I definitely recommend it for what little time and money it costs. Also, this comes with a gig bag! I've bought guitars from music shops that don't even come with that much! All in all, definitely worth it for the price!

The guitar would not tune properly or even hold the tuning. Intonation was non-exsistent. People always want something for less. In the case of instruments you get what you pay for. Keep in mind that a good set of acoustic guitar strings runs around $12-18. So how much quality could this be for that price? As a fun toy great...an instrument not so much.

The product description is misleading.  As advertised, there are stickers for an 88 key piano.  However, the problem is that beyond 2 octives, this brand eliminated the notes on the staffs.  Just a sticker with the alphabet letter on it only.  I thought I would try to write in the notes and staffs myself, but it was too much work and it didn't look very good.  I pulled them all off,  bought a new black and white set complete with all the notes and staffs for 88 keys. The colored stickers might catch your attention when considering a purchase, but you won't get what you're paying for.

I really wanted to love this wooden recorder, but after many attempts to achieve an acceptable, consistent sound quality from it, I decided to return it.

I purchased this three-piece recorder with baroque fingering and half holes. It’s made of solid maple wood with a beautiful finish. It appears to be well made; however, the overall sound consistency was flawed, making it frustrating to play. I’m new to the recorder, but not new to woodwind instruments. I played the closed hole, transverse flute for many years in a large orchestra setting as first chair. So I’m experienced with the importance of achieving a flexible embouchure for sound quality. Effective air flow on the recorder is much easier to achieve, and breathing from the diaphragm has become natural to me. So I honestly don’t think my air flow was the problem. I know it takes time to properly “break-in” a wooden instrument; but by then, it would be too late to return it. So I opted to focus my need to make music on a penny whistle, as well as an alto recorder from Yamaha, both of which I bought here on Amazon. I just want to learn something new to play now that I’m retired!

I'm an elementary band director and I've had several students show up with these instruments. Please trust me - I've probably had 20 kids with Mendinis and have a lot more experience than one parent who (at the time of a review) had a positive outlook. Band directors all cringe at the name of Mendini, or any other of the cheap Amazon instruments. They. Do. Not. Last.

1. Really, they do not last. I've never had a student play on one for more than a year. They break all the time. Constantly. Weekly, sometimes. Because they are cheap, noname instruments, they can't be fixed by repair shops because replacement parts don't exist and/or don't work. Many repair shops refuse to even try to fix it because even if it could be fixed, the metal is so thin, cheap, and brittle that it will either break or bend right back. Kids get frustrated when their instrument is never working and then quit.

2. The tone is horrible. Absolutely horrible. This is not me being a picky band director - the kids even notice it and point it out. Nobody wants their kid to be singled out because they are doing some poorly - and it's not even the kid's fault!

3. The tuning is extremely off. This is noticeable by the kids as well. It's to the point where the instrument is playing a completely different note than the one that it is supposed to sound like. No amount of adjustment from the kid or teacher will fix this. Your child will stick out and be embarrassed.

4. No resale value. The instrument will not last long enough to even have value after a few months, but even if it did, it will not hold value. Good instruments are atill valuable years after - people are still playing on instruments from the 40s. In a few months, you will be stuck with a perpetually broken instrument that either you will pour money in to trying to fix it, or that is so broken it can't be fixed and then you are flat out of that money.

I understand; band is expensive. But you could take this money and find a used instrument - a good brand- and end up with something that will WORK! If that's not an option, talk to your school's band director to see if they have school instruments available.

Trust me - this is NOT band director approved. We call Mendinis 'instrument shaped objects' because that is exactly all they are.

This amp is amazing, & the presets are honestly super cool. Looks nice & sounds great. Plus the bag it comes with is SUPER handy. I’ve only had 2 issues thus far that could be isolated to just my unit, or is a bug that can be patched in future updates.

[1st ISSUE]:
Every single time I turn this amp on & want to use the app. I have to wait 5min for it to connect if I just wait. I figured out a workaround to waiting 5min+ (not exaggerating) for the app to find the speaker; here’s my process:

1) turn speaker on
2) press “connect” on the app
3) press “retry” after it says it “failed to connect”
4) press “cancel” because it’ll take 5min otherwise
5) press “connect,” after 10 seconds, it finally connects.

Hopefully this helps anyone else who has this issue, because it took me a few weeks to figure it out.

[2nd ISSUE]:
The tuner is unreliable. It had me tune my A string crazy low, so I had to whip out a tuning app on my phone, which told me it was -9. Needless to say, I’m disappointed a free app is better than the tuner in this $300 amp

For reference:
• The Spark app is updated to latest version
• I tried the app on my iPhone 11 Pro Max & iPad Air 4th gen, both using the latest version of iOS
• BlueTooth on my iPhone & iPad work just fine (no other devices like AirPods, keyboards, or anything else have this issue)
• I’ve updated the firmware on the amp itself by following the video tutorial Spark posted on YouTube.
• The app takes forever to connect 100% of the time if I don’t intervene.

Otherwise I love this amp & I can’t imagine a better one for this price. If it wasn’t for the app connection being a huge task every time, I’d give this 6 stars. Honestly now that I’m used to it, it’s not so bad, but when I first got it, I was furious.

Junk.
I'm an elementary band director and I've had several students show up with these instruments. Please trust me - I've probably had 20 kids with Mendinis and have a lot more experience than one parent who (at the time of a review) had a positive outlook. Band directors all cringe at the name of Mendini, or any other of the cheap Amazon instruments. They. Do. Not. Last.

1. Really, they do not last. I've never had a student play on one for more than a year. They break all the time. Constantly. Weekly, sometimes. Because they are cheap, noname instruments, they can't be fixed by repair shops because replacement parts don't exist and/or don't work. Many repair shops refuse to even try to fix it because even if it could be fixed, the metal is so thin, cheap, and brittle that it will either break or bend right back. Kids get frustrated when their instrument is never working and then quit.

2. The tone is horrible. Absolutely horrible. This is not me being a picky band director - the kids even notice it and point it out. Nobody wants their kid to be singled out because they are doing some poorly - and it's not even the kid's fault!

3. The tuning is extremely off. This is noticeable by the kids as well. It's to the point where the instrument is playing a completely different note than the one that it is supposed to sound like. No amount of adjustment from the kid or teacher will fix this. Your child will stick out and be embarrassed.

4. No resale value. The instrument will not last long enough to even have value after a few months, but even if it did, it will not hold value. Good instruments are atill valuable years after - people are still playing on instruments from the 40s. In a few months, you will be stuck with a perpetually broken instrument that either you will pour money in to trying to fix it, or that is so broken it can't be fixed and then you are flat out of that money.

I understand; band is expensive. But you could take this money and find a used instrument - a good brand- and end up with something that will WORK! If that's not an option, talk to your school's band director to see if they have school instruments available.

Trust me - this is NOT band director approved. We call Mendinis 'instrument shaped objects' because that is exactly all they are.

If you ignore all of my advice, at least do your kid one favor. Get the clarinet in black. If you get it in the fun color, the kids will only be able to identify who is making the bad sounds even faster.

I bought two of these for my daughters, age 4 and 6, so they could each have one to play. So I want to start off by saying both instruments were pretty and sounded very nice. Everything came as described and pictured. I thought they were very reasonably priced for having a hard carrying case.
Here was my issue and reason for returning. In the accessory pouch (a little mesh drawstring bag) was a thick cardboard thank you card saturated with perfume. So everything smells like perfume (instrument, carrying case, all the accessories) and since the perfume card touched everything in the case everything has perfume on it. Not only did it give me a headache (not a good smelling perfume and way too much) but I was concerned as I bought to give to my daughters and my oldest daughter has moderate to severe asthma and I really think it would cause an asthma attack. To put it perspective the amount of perfume involved...I removed the perfume cardboard pieces and sat them aside, washed my hands twice, and I still have the perfume smell on my hands. I do not believe it will air out and I do not believe it will come off the cleaning cloth or the liner of the carrying case being that they're fabric and I'm not so sure the smell would come off the instrument either. So I am returning them and ordering another brand which hopefully does not have a perfume cardboard thank you card.
It is O.K. Just a little cheap looking but for the price I paid I guess it does make a good conversation piece.

"8.2" bendable and flexible gooseneck stand included in the bundle" - this is not what comes with the microphone. It comes with a 'lightweight desk stand' that cannot hold the weight of the microphone, much less the shockmount and pop filter. The pictures show the gooseneck holding the mic and shock filter but the stand that comes with absolutely can't. Disappointed, can't set up my mic until a new stand arrives. The pop filter is also just wide metal screen, not micro mesh and with no fiber filter at all. If you're buying this as a bundle, save your money.
